headline: The wins prove the plan works — the Caro ...e5 break lost both losses
note: Four evening games, 2W-2L, and the day split cleanly. The wins ran the plan: London win vs kELANASTASIA castled on move 8 and finished with 4:07 on the clock — your best time usage yet — and the clachesss game broke the 1.d4 curse with a King's Indian setup, castling on move 5 and delivering a back-rank mate (30...Rb1#), the very pattern that keeps killing you. Both losses were the same Caro-Kann wound: White played Bb5, you answered with a premature ...e5 break (vs keroRoma and callmetaide), lost a pawn to the Bxc6-then-Nxe5 removed-defender trick, and the king never got safe — vs keroRoma you never castled and were mated Re8# on move 22. Fix: in the Caro, develop and castle BEFORE breaking with ...e5, and when a rook shares the e-file with your king, castling is the most urgent move on the board.

focus.title: Caro-Kann vs Bb5: castle before you break
focus.detail: Both losses came from the same Bb5 position where ...e5 dropped a pawn and exposed the king; drill the line to a safe castled middlegame.

tasks.title: Caro-Kann vs Bb5: castle before you break
tasks.detail: Both evening losses (keroRoma, callmetaide) started 5.Bb5 and both times ...e5 lost a pawn to Bxc6 then Nxe5. Drill the repertoire line: develop, castle, then break. The Caro SRS line is also 0-for-2.

tasks.title: Back-rank scan — you proved you see it
tasks.detail: You mated clachesss with Rb1# on the back rank, then keroRoma mated YOU with Re8# the same way. The pattern is in your eyes as attacker; run the scan as defender every move.

tasks.title: K+R mate: get the first success
tasks.detail: Still 0-for-8 on the king-and-rook mate drill. One clean success unlocks the box method — it is the last step of every conversion.
